Abstract
--------

Information and communication technologies hold immense potential to enhance our lives and societal well-being. However, digital spaces have also emerged as a fertile ground for fake news campaigns and hate speech, aggravating polarization and posing a threat to societal harmony. Despite the fact that this dark side is acknowledged in the literature, the complexity of polarization as a phenomenon coupled with the socio-technical nature of fake news necessitates a novel approach to unravel its intricacies. In light of this sophistication, the current study employs complexity theory and a configurational approach to investigate the impact of diverse disinformation campaigns and hate speech in polarizing societies across 177 countries through a cross-country investigation. The results demonstrate the definitive role of disinformation and hate speech in polarizing societies. The findings also offer a balanced perspective on internet censorship and social media monitoring as necessary evils to combat the disinformation menace and control polarization, but suggest that such efforts may lend support to a milieu of hate speech that fuels polarization. Implications for theory and practice are discussed.

**Keywords:** Polarization, Disinformation, Fake news, Hate speech, Internet censorship, Social media monitoring

Introduction
------------

Information and communication technologies (ICT) hold immense potential for people and societal advancement in a multitude of avenues (Bentley et al., [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR17); Parthasarathy & Ramamritham, [2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR129); Saha et al., [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR151)). However, on the flipside, ICTs also constitute a threat to society (Ahmed et al., [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR2)), and digital spaces have been accused of providing a fertile ground for fake news campaigns and online hate speech (Aanestad et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR1); Castaño-Pulgarín et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR31)), while exacerbating societal polarization (Törnberg, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR171)). Polarization is a social phenomenon characterized by the fragmentation of society into antagonistic factions with vehemently opposed values and identities that impede cooperation and the pursuit of a common good (Stewart et al., [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR163)). The threat is significant, and democracies globally are under siege and reeling from the impact of polarized societies. According to the latest World Economic Forum study on global risks, societal polarization is not just one of the world’s top 10 present concerns, but also a long-term threat over the next decade (Zahidi, [2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR183)). The phenomenon has impeded pandemic response in countries (Stuenkel, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR165)), slowed consensus on critical global issues such as climate change (Sparkman et al., [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR161)), and continues to challenge the resilience of societies, resulting in a catastrophic loss of diversity in the social fabric (Kelly, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR90)). Not only does polarization increase the likelihood of violence in societies (Piazza, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR136)), but violent demonstrations may exacerbate polarization and divide individuals along partisan lines (De Salle, [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR51)). These devastating effects of polarization have catapulted it to a top priority for scholars across disciplines seeking an understanding of the causes of this social evil (Stewart et al., [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR163)).

Information technology and social media, in particular, play a crucial role in fostering polarization (Arora et al., [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR12); Qureshi et al., [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR139)), with a spillover effect on offline violence and protests (Gallacher et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR67)). These platforms are not merely spaces of public discourse, but also raise concerns over free speech, particularly when they contribute to the online amplification of the most radical and polarizing content (Riemer & Peter, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR146)). Debates on free speech on platforms predominantly revolve around disinformation and hate speech (Riemer & Peter, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR146)), both of which are considered to exacerbate polarization (Meneguelli & Ferré-Pavia, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR111); WEF, [2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR178)). While governments and platforms pursue content moderation and censorship to combat this menace of fake news and hate speech (Riemer & Peter, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR146); Vese, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR174)), such practices are regarded as a threat to free speech (Vese, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR174)) and contribute to polarization (Frasz, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR63)).

The role of fake news and hate speech in polarizing societies is widely acknowledged in prior literature (Au et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR14); Piazza, [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR135)). Its crippling effects are now accompanied by rising calls for action on online disinformation, which threatens to polarize society and destroy democratic life (The Express Tribune, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR169)). Fake news as a term gained prominence during the U.S. presidential elections of 2016 (Allcott & Gentzkow, [2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR5)). Ever since, the issue has persisted and poses a grave threat to the societal fabric (Olan et al., [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR118)). The term overlaps with other forms of information disorder such as misinformation and disinformation (Lazer et al., [2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR96)). While the former refers to false or misleading information (Lazer et al., [2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR96)), the latter is a deliberate fabrication and dissemination of falsehood with an intent to deceive (Zimdars & Mcleod, [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR187)). In contrast to falsehood, hate speech refers to the use of offensive language directed against a specific group of individuals who share a common characteristic (Castaño-Pulgarín et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR31)).

Politics remains a favored breeding ground for disinformation and hate speech (Freelon & Wells, [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR64); Piazza, [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR135)). While social media and online platforms have played a pivotal role in amplifying the spread of disinformation and hate speech (Meske & Bunde, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR113); Olan et al., [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR118)), recent reports reveal the rampant misuse of these platforms by governments and political parties in over eighty countries engaged in political disinformation campaigns (Alba & Satariano, [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR3); Oxford Internet Institute, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR122)). Likewise there are global concerns over hate speech “super-charged by the internet” in allowing falsehood and conspiracy theories to proliferate and provoke offline violence (United Nations, [2023](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR173)).

While social networking sites (SNSes) have started to ramp up efforts to detect and curb this menace (Hatmaker, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR76); O’Regan & Theil, [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR120)), governments have also stepped up measures to actively monitor social media using sophisticated technology that enables them to monitor citizens’ behaviors on these platforms (Shahbaz & Funk, [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR156)). Some governments have pushed the envelope further by imposing censorship on the internet (Ovide, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR121)). Such measures challenge the freedom of expression online (González-Quiñones & Machin-Mastromatteo, [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR71)) and contribute to polarization and political animosity among citizens (Frasz, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR63)).

In light of the fact that the discourse on free speech online is embroiled in a conflict with surveillance and censorship efforts, even as disinformation and hate speech proliferate on these platforms, we assert that these core factors in the free speech debate merit exploration regarding their combinatorial effect on polarization as a phenomenon. This significance stems from the fact that extant literature has predominantly engaged in linear investigations of polarization as a phenomenon. In this context, several studies have analyzed the relationship between polarization and factors such as internet and social media usage (Boxell et al., [2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR20)), democratic erosion and media censorship (Arbatli & Rosenberg, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR8)), media exposure (Hawdon et al., [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR77)), fake news (Piazza, [2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR136)), and hate speech (Piazza, [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR135)). However, the vast majority of studies have examined factors in isolation and assumed linear relationships between phenomena.[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#Fn1) Political processes are predominantly non-linear in nature (Richards & Doyle, [2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR144); Timofeev, [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR170)), and recent literature emphasizes the importance of non-linearity in appreciating aspects that might explain and alleviate polarization (Kelly, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR90); Leonard et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR97)).

Polarization is characterized by asymmetry and constitutes a collection of complex, ever-evolving systems, the complexity of which necessitates a novel approach to unravel its intricacies (Kelly, [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR90); Leonard et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR97); Levin et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR99)). In addition, the socio-technical character of fake news makes standard methods of investigation intrinsically complex (Au et al., [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#CR14)). In light of this complexity, this paper acknowledges the phenomenon’s sophistication and leans on complexity theory and configurational analysis in an effort to decipher the causal pathways that influence societal polarization. In particular, this study seeks to illustrate how various sources of political disinformation, hate speech, social media monitoring efforts, and online censorship influence the degree of polarization in societies. In lieu of focusing on the main effects between polarization and its causes, the current study seeks to identify the configurations that explain societal polarization. Consequently, this study addresses the following research question:

*   **_RQ_**: What configurations of political disinformation, hate speech, social media monitoring, and internet censorship lead to polarization in societies?

This configural narrative towards the conceptualization of polarization lends credence to recent assertions regarding the impact of fake news in fracturing societies and intensifying polarization. Specifically, the study’s findings point to a balanced view of surveillance and censorship by governments, which may be useful in combating disinformation campaigns on social media and controlling polarization, although such efforts may lend support to a milieu of hate speech that fuels polarization. Additionally, identification of configurations as causal pathways to polarization should serve as crucial insights to social media platform players in recognizing the varying manifestations of disinformation, hate speech and monitoring efforts to deal with societal polarization, while the findings of this study should also assist political scientists and complex systems theorists with a more nuanced understanding of the dynamics of polarization in society.

The paper is structured as follows. In the next section, we review the literature on polarization, political disinformation, hate speech, internet censorship, and social media monitoring and develop the conceptual model based on complexity theory which serves as the theoretical foundation for the study. Section[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#Sec8) discusses the research methodology and measures utilized for analysis. Section[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#Sec11) describes the process of data analysis using the configurational approach. Section[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#Sec20) analyzes the empirical results, and Section[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#Sec21) discusses the implications to theory and practice. Section[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#Sec25) highlights limitations alongside discussing future research directions before concluding in Section[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10106894/#Sec26).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Misinformation spreads rapidly on social media, creating false narratives that can mislead public opinion and influence political discourse, as seen in the 2020 election.
False information alters public perception, often leading to distrust in legitimate news sources and skewing voter beliefs, contributing to polarized political environments.
Widespread misinformation erodes public trust in democratic institutions, causing citizens to question electoral integrity and governance, ultimately diminishing civic engagement.
The 2020 U.S. election saw rampant misinformation, including claims of widespread voter fraud, which severely impacted public confidence in the electoral process and outcomes.
